# Zeta Based Functions

To describe zeta, you need to use zeta itself. <br />
Define zeta‑based functions as symmetric combinations of ζ

```math
\text{zsinh}(z, t) = \frac{\zeta(z + t) - \zeta(-z + t)}{2}
```

```math
\text{zcosh}(z, t) = \frac{\zeta(z + t) + \zeta(-z + t)}{2}
```

<br />

```math
zsinh(−z, t) = −zsinh(z, t)
```

```math
zcosh(−z, t) = zcosh(z, t)
```

<br />

```math
\zeta(-z + t) = zcosh(z, t) - zsinh(z, t)
```

```math
\zeta(z + t) = zcosh(z, t) + zsinh(z, t)
```

<br />

```math
zeta(0) = zcosh(0, 0) = -1/2
```

<br />

```math
\zeta(z + t)\zeta(-z + t) = \text{zcosh}^2(z, t) - \text{zsinh}^2(z, t)
```

Graphics zeros zsinh (green), zcosh (blue), t = 0
<img src="https://asyncker.github.io/zeta-trigonometry/img/zeros-zsin-zcos.png">

Graphics subtract zeros zsinh (green), zcosh (blue), t = 0
<img src="https://asyncker.github.io/zeta-trigonometry/img/zeros-zsin-zcos-sub.png">

The zeros of zsinh(z, t) lie where `Im(ζ(z))` <br />
The zeros of zcosh(z, t) lie where `Re(ζ(z))` <br />
The zeros of f(x) lie where `Im(ζ(iz) * ζ(-iz))` <br />

<br />

```python
def find_zeros():
    step = prev = cur = lastzero = 0.0
    while True:
        step += 0.01
        t = step + 0.5j
        if prev * cur < 0.0:
            zerot = findroot(lambda x: im(zeta(1j * x) * zeta(-x * 1j)), (t - 0.01, t))
            if (lastzero == zerot):
                continue
            lastzero = zerot
            print(zerot)
        prev = cur
        cur = im(zeta(1j * t) * zeta(-t * 1j))

find_zeros()
```

<br />

If we take the derivative of zsinh using exp, then the function becomes non-closed. The question is, what will the exp derivative tend to if we take it an infinite number of times from zsinh?

```math
L(z) =  \frac{\zeta'(z)}{\zeta(z)}
```

Hypothesis:
The roots of the zeta-cosine function consistently become greater than the roots of the zeta-sine function. Does this always hold true for t<1/2? For t=1/2, this rule is violated.

t = 1/2 - 0.00001
```
3.4362140094042727618286113340360934506738787548597
```

```js
    for (let i = 1; i < 30001; i++) {
        console.log(zsinh_zeros[i] <= zcosh_zeros[i]); // 30k all true except first
    }
```

See graphics on https://asyncker.github.io/zeta-trigonometry/index.html

<br />12-adic numbers<br />

```math
\zeta(-1) = -\frac{1}{12} = -0.1_{12}
```

```math
\zeta(0) = -\frac{1}{2} = -0.6_{12}
```

```math
\zeta(2) = \frac{\pi^2}{6} = \pi^2 \cdot 0.2_{12}
```

<br />Non-Archimedean<br />
w^-1 = eps <br />
eps^-1 = w <br />
w * eps = 1 <br />

```
ln(eps) = -ln(w)
ln(w) = -ln(eps)

ln(w^eps) = -eps * ln(eps)
ln(eps^w) = -w * ln(w)

ln(eps^eps) = -eps * ln(w)
ln(w^w) = -w * ln(eps)

ln(eps * w) = 0
ln(w) / ln(eps) = -1

|eps * (2 + 2i)| = |eps| * |2 + 2i| = eps * 2.828...

a = 2^n * 5^m (all non-periodic fractions for the 10-adic)

3 * 0.333 + 0.001 = 1.0
3 * 0.333333... + eps = 1.0

(w - 1) * 1/w = 0.999999...
(1 - eps) * 1/eps = 999999... 999.000000...
999999... + 0.999999... = 999999... 999.999999... 
```

Scale plane (x-asis it's mult y-asis it's power (example x * y^2)):<br />
The x-axis is the multiplied coefficient for eps or w  <br />
<img src="https://github.com/asyncker/zeta-trigonometry/blob/main/img/scale_plane_algebra.png">
